claim: The deformation behaviour of binary monocrystalline and polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys with Al contents up to 18 at.% was studied, with impurity carbon content less than 100 wt.ppm.
evidence_text: The deformation behaviour of binary monocrystalline and polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys with Al contents up to 18 at.% and only low unavoidable impurity contents—in particular less than 100 wt.ppm C—has been studied at room temperature and −100 °C.

claim: At higher Al contents beginning with about 10 at.% Al there is a transition from the disordered atom distribution to the K-state, and then to long-range ordering to produce the D0₃ and B2 crystal structures with increasing Al content.
evidence_text: At such high Al contents there is a transition from the disordered atom distribution to an intermediate state, which is known as K-state, and then to long-range ordering to produce the D0₃ crystal structure and the B2 crystal structure with increasing Al content.

claim: Binary Fe-Al alloys with Al contents in the range 4–18% Al were prepared by vacuum induction melting in Al₂O₃ crucibles using Fe with 99.99% purity and Al with 99.999% purity and solidification in Cu moulds to obtain rods usually with 18 mm diameter.
evidence_text: Binary Fe-Al alloys with Al contents in the range 4–18% Al (always at.% if not stated otherwise) were prepared by vacuum induction melting in Al₂O₃ crucibles using Fe with 99.99% purity (purified by zone melting to minimise the impurity content) and Al with 99.999% purity and solidification in Cu moulds to obtain rods usually with 18 mm diameter.

claim: The monocrystals were etched by ammonium persulfate ((NH₄)₂S₂O₈) and oriented using a special laser light-figure method.
evidence_text: The monocrystals were etched by ammonium persulfate ((NH₄)₂S₂O₈) and oriented using a special laser light-figure method [21].

claim: Specimens were heat-treated at temperatures up to 600 °C usually for less than 14 days, at higher temperatures less than 4 days and at 1100 °C usually only 15 min; heat treatments up to 300 °C occurred in an argon atmosphere, others in evacuated SiO₂ capsules.
evidence_text: The specimens were heat-treated at temperatures up to 600 °C usually for less than 14 days, at higher temperatures less than 4 days and at 1100 °C usually only 15 min. The short heat treatments at temperatures up to 300 °C occurred in an argon atmosphere with 99.99% purity. For all other heat treatments the specimens were enclosed in evacuated (10⁻⁶ bar) SiO₂ capsules.

claim: Microhardness was measured with the Fischerscope H100 under a maximum load of 1 N, determining the universal hardness HU as HU = F/(26.43·h²) [N/mm²], and reported HU values are averages of more than 40 measurements.
evidence_text: the microhardness tester Fischerscope H100 (with maximum load of 1 N) was used which allows the determination of the elastic and plastic deformation. The respective so-called universal hardness HU was determined as HU = F/(26.43·h²) [N/mm²] with the indentation force F and the indentation depth h. The reported HU values are averages of more than 40 measurements.

claim: The yield stress was determined as 0.2% proof stress in compression (with total straining of at least 5%) at room temperature and −100 °C at strain rates of 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ and 10⁻² s⁻¹ using specimens of dimensions 5 × 5 × 10 mm³.
evidence_text: The yield stress was determined as 0.2% proof stress in compression (with total straining of at least 5%) at room temperature and −100 °C with rates of 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ and 10⁻² s⁻¹. The surfaces of the specimens with dimensions 5 × 5 × 10 mm³ were cleansed by grinding and the monocrystals were polished with 3 µm diamond paste before testing.

claim: Ductility was studied by tensile testing at strain rates between 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ and 10⁻² s⁻¹ using cylindrical specimens prepared according to German standard DIN 50125.
evidence_text: Ductility was studied by tensile testing at rates between 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ and 10⁻² s⁻¹. For this, cylindrical specimens were prepared by turning according to German standard DIN 50125.

claim: Microstructural characterization was performed by optical microscopy to reveal grain boundaries and precipitates, SEM with EDX for fracture surfaces and qualitative precipitate analysis, and TEM using a Philips CM20 instrument at 200 kV with thin foils prepared by electrolytic twin jet polishing and ion milling.
evidence_text: The specimens were etched by an alcoholic 10% nitric acid solution for metallographic inspection by optical microscopy for revealing grain boundaries and precipitates. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used for studying fracture surfaces and for qualitative precipitate analysis (by energy-dispersive X-ray diffraction (EDX)). For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a Philips CM20 instrument with 200 kV thin foils were prepared by electrolytic twin jet polishing at –30 °C and 12 V voltage using a 7:3 mixture of methanole and nitric acid. In addition, ion milling was applied if necessary.

claim: Any uptake of Si by the possible reaction of the specimens with the SiO₂ capsules could not be detected for the chosen heat-treatment conditions.
evidence_text: Any uptake of Si by the possible reaction of the specimens with the SiO₂ capsules could not be detected for the chosen heat-treatment conditions.

claim: Fine thin platelike or rod-like carbide precipitates formed on grain boundaries after slow furnace cooling from 1100 °C or prolonged heat treatments of 14 days at 320 °C, and the amount decreased with lower carbon content; only rare tiny precipitates were detected in an alloy with 20 wt.ppm C after furnace cooling.
evidence_text: Fine thin platelike or rod-like precipitates were revealed by metallographic inspection on grain boundaries after slow furnace cooling from $1 1 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ or prolonged heat treatments of 14 days at $3 2 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ which are believed to be carbides due to the presence of up to 80 ppm carbon. Indeed the amount of these precipitated particles was smaller for lower C contents and only rare tiny precipitate particles were detected on grain boundaries of an alloy with only 20 ppm C after furnace cooling.

claim: Noticeable short-range ordering occurs in Fe-Al alloys with 13–18 at.% Al at temperatures below 600 °C during cooling after solidification, and quenching from 1100 °C suppresses it nearly completely.
evidence_text: It was found that noticeable short-range ordering occurs in Fe-Al alloys with Al contents in the range 13–18% (always at%) at temperatures below 600 °C during cooling after solidification. ... Quenching from 1100 °C suppressed short-range ordering nearly completely.

claim: The highest degree of short-range ordering was observed at 250 °C during isochronal annealing of Fe-18%Al for 14 days, with an activation energy of 0.3 eV/atom, and 100 h at 250 °C were sufficient to reach a near-equilibrium state.
evidence_text: The highest degree of short-range ordering (as characterised by x-ray scattering intensity) was observed at 250 °C (for isochronal annealings of Fe-18%Al for 14 days). The activation energy for this ordering reaction was found as only 0.3 eV/atom and indeed 100 h were sufficient at 250 °C for reaching a near-equilibrium state.

claim: Microhardness of Fe-16%Al monocrystals is insensitive to short-range ordering: treatments producing maximum ordering (250 °C/14 days), minimum ordering (quench from 600 °C or 1100 °C), and intermediate ordering (furnace cooling, 300 °C/6 h, 300 °C/3 days) all yield the same hardness as the as-grown crystal, indicating that short-range ordering has no major impact on hardness.
evidence_text: Both the 250 °C/14 day anneal for maximum ordering and the quench from 600 °C ... for minimum ordering as well as furnace cooling from 1100 °C to room temperature and anneals at 300 °C for 6 h and 3 day with intermediate ordering produce the same hardness which equals that of the as-grown monocrystal. ... It is concluded that short-range ordering has no major impact on hardness.

claim: Stress–strain curves (Fig. 2) show that only heat treatments at temperatures above 600 °C significantly affect the mechanical behaviour; as-grown and furnace-cooled materials differ only little from material furnace-cooled to 600 °C followed by quenching, while quenching from 1100 °C with subsequent anneal produces a higher yield stress.
evidence_text: The stress-strain curves in Fig. 2 show a similar behaviour. ... It is concluded that only heat treatments at temperatures above 600 °C affect the mechanical behaviour of Fe-Al alloys significantly.

claim: Quenched-in excess vacancies are immobile at low temperatures and act as effective obstacles to dislocation motion, contributing to hardening; the highest hardness in Fig. 1 is shown by the specimen quenched from 1100 °C to room temperature.
evidence_text: Excess vacancies at low temperatures are immobile, i.e. they are effective obstacles to dislocation movement and contribute to hardening ... Indeed the highest hardness is shown in Fig. 1 by the specimen which was quenched from 1100 °C to room temperature.

claim: In Fe-40%Al (B2 ordered), hardness and yield stress increase steeply with increasing quench temperature above 500 °C, attributed to vacancy hardening, and a linear relationship exists between microhardness and the square root of the vacancy concentration.
evidence_text: Similar effects have been observed for Fe-Al alloys with much higher Al-content, i.e. for Fe-40%Al which is an ordered intermetallic phase with B2 structure ... Both the hardness and the yield stress were found to increase steeply with increasing quench temperature for quench temperatures above 500 °C. ... a linear relationship between the microhardness and the square root of the vacancy concentration was observed for such alloys with 40–50%Al [28].

claim: In Fe-Al alloys with Al up to 20 at.%, excess vacancies heal out at temperatures between 300 and 400 °C; however, the healing kinetics are slow, and annealing at 300 °C even for 14 days may not completely eliminate excess vacancies.
evidence_text: In the case of Fe-Al alloys with Al contents up to 20% excess vacancies were reported to heal out at temperatures between 300 and 400 °C ... This may indicate rather slow kinetics of the healing out of excess vacancies. ... This would mean that the annealing at 300 °C ... even for 14 days may not have been sufficient for the complete elimination of the excess vacancies.

claim: Earlier data by Morgand et al. for polycrystalline alloys (C up to 120 ppm) show a linear increase of yield stress with Al content with a hardening coefficient of about 1.8 GPa at 100 °C for Al contents up to 18%.
evidence_text: Earlier data by Morgand et al. for polycrystalline alloys (with C contents up to 120 ppm) also show a linear increase of the yield stress with increasing Al content with a hardening coefficient of about 1.8 GPa at 100 °C for Al contents up to 18% [2].

claim: The deviations from the linear yield stress behavior at −100 °C, visible in Fig. 5(b), are attributed to additional deformation twinning.
evidence_text: The deviations from the linear behaviour at −100 °C, which are visible in Fig. 5(b), are attributed to additional deformation twinning.

claim: The fracture strain reaches the elongation before necking at 18% Al at room temperature and at 14% Al at −100 °C, indicating a transition to brittle fracture without necking.
evidence_text: At higher Al contents the fracture strain decreases with increasing Al content more steeply to reach the elongation before necking at 18%Al at room temperature and at 14%Al at −100 °C.

claim: There is a transition from ductile fracture with necking to brittle fracture without necking for Al contents in the range of 15–18% Al at room temperature and of 12–14% Al at −100 °C.
evidence_text: Obviously there is a transition from ductile fracture with necking to brittle fracture without necking for Al contents in the range of 15–18%Al at room temperature and of 12–14%Al at −100 °C.

claim: Yield stress drops were produced only by Fe-4%Al and Fe-6%Al at −100 °C, as indicated by Fig. 4(b).
evidence_text: Yield stress drops were produced only by Fe-4%Al (Fig. 4(b)) and Fe-6%Al at −100 °C.

claim: The yield stress drops observed at −100 °C in as-cast alloys with low Al contents (4 and 6% Al) may be related to excess vacancies, but more detailed studies are needed.
evidence_text: It is concluded that the yield stress drops, which were observed at −100 °C for the as-cast alloys with low Al contents, may be related to excess vacancies. However, more detailed studies are necessary for clarifying this and providing clear evidences.

claim: Deformation twinning was found at room temperature only for Fe-18%Al (Fig. 3). At −100 °C, deformation twinning occurred for Al contents in the range of 11–18%.
evidence_text: In the present case of Fe-Al alloys, deformation twinning was found at room temperature only for the Fe-18%Al alloy (Fig. 3), which agrees with early observations [51,53]. At −100 °C deformation twinning occurred already for lower Al contents in the range of 11–18%.

claim: In Fe-Al alloys, deformation twinning was found at room temperature only for the Fe-18%Al alloy, while at −100 °C it occurred for Al contents in the range of 11–18%.
evidence_text: deformation twinning was found at room temperature only for the Fe-18%Al alloy (Fig. 3), which agrees with early observations [51,53]. At −100 °C deformation twinning occurred already for lower Al contents in the range of 11–18%.

claim: The deformation behaviour of binary Fe-Al alloys with Al contents up to 18 at.% and less than 100 wt.ppm C was studied at room temperature and −100 °C.
evidence_text: The deformation behaviour of variously treated binary Fe-Al alloys with Al contents up to 18 at.% and only low unavoidable impurity contents—in particular less than 100 wt.ppm C—has been studied at room temperature and −100 °C.

claim: The yield stress at room temperature increases linearly with increasing Al content up to 18 at.% Al, corresponding to Suzuki's theory of solid-solution hardening for bcc alloys, including alloys with short-range order.
evidence_text: The yield stress of the studied alloys at room temperature increases linearly with increasing Al content up to 18 at.% Al which corresponds to Suzuki's theory of solid-solution hardening for bcc alloys. This composition range includes alloys with short-range order.

claim: Ductility characterized by elongation before necking and fracture strain decreases with increasing Al content; ductile fracture occurs up to about 15 at.% Al at room temperature and up to about 12 at.% Al at −100 °C, with a transition to brittle fracture without necking in the range of 15–18 at.% Al at RT and 12–14 at.% Al at −100 °C.
evidence_text: Corresponding to the increasing yield stress by solid-solution hardening, the ductility as characterised by elongation before necking and fracture strain decreases with increasing Al content with ductile fracture for Al contents up to about 15 at.% Al at room temperature and to about 12 at.% Al at −100 °C and a transition to brittle fracture without necking in the range of 15–18 at.% Al at room temperature and of 12–14 at.% Al at −100 °C.
